- orient: orient
- autoresearch: autoresearch
- well-known-agent: Probe an A2A-style agent discovery endpoint at /.well-known/agent.json — fetch, validate shape, smoke-test advertised capabilities, and report findings
- freeqcc:freeqcc-launch: Launch a freeqcc agent — a freeq-DM-controllable Claude Code session with a cryptographic did:key identity, owner-DID-gated access, and a declarative provenance cert. Use when the user wants to start their agent and become reachable via Bluesky DM.
- freeqcc:freeqcc-stop: Stop the running freeqcc daemon. Sends SIGTERM; the daemon does a clean QUIT (PRESENCE :state=offline + IRC QUIT) before exiting. Use when the user wants to take their agent offline.
- freeqcc:freeqcc-status: Show the live state of the user's freeqcc daemon: connected? owner verified? bot DID/nick? provenance status? Use when the user asks "is my agent up?" or wants a quick health check.
- alexandria:complete-plan: Close out an executed implementation plan. Reviews an existing release doc plus outcomes and tickets, determines what shipped versus what was deferred, captures decisions made during execution, and updates release.md with completion status and retrospective learnings. Use after a plan has been executed and needs a durable close-out artifact.
- alexandria:alexandria-upgrade: Upgrade Alexandria to the latest version. Detects install type (git clone vs vendored), pulls updates, and verifies the upgrade. Use when the update check reports a new version is available, or when asked to "upgrade the context library" or "update the plugin".
- alexandria:context-briefing: Assemble context from Alexandria for implementation tasks. Use when starting a feature, fixing a bug, or making changes that touch product concepts in the library.
- alexandria:implementation-planning: Create an implementation plan for a product goal. Takes a goal, gathers context from the context library, proposes success outcomes with scope tiers, and produces dependency-ordered tickets as markdown files. Use when asked to "plan a feature", "break down into tickets", "create an implementation plan", or "plan the release". Proactively suggest when a user describes a large feature they want to build.
- alexandria:wizard: Meet Raven and build your product knowledge layer together. Configures your Alexandria through conversation about your product, team, and AI usage — producing a prioritized seeding plan that tells Conan and Sam what knowledge to build first.
- alexandria:revise-plan: Revise an existing implementation plan when re-planning triggers fire. Reviews the release doc plus affected outcomes and tickets, incorporates completed enabler findings or milestone learnings, identifies remaining tickets that need revision, and updates the plan artifacts in place. Use mid-flight when new evidence changes the execution plan.
- alexandria:sync-tickets: Preview and sync Alexandria plan tickets to GitHub issues using the existing sync CLI. Use after planning work is written to disk and you want to create or update the corresponding GitHub issues without leaving the conversation.
- alexandria:library: Your persistent library configuration room. Raven reads your current library state and picks up where you left off.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Cryptographic Audit Report: freeq-sdk

### Finding 1: No Zeroization of Secret Key Material

**Severity: HIGH**

**Description:** None of the private key types (`PrivateKey`, `IdentityKeyPair`, `SignedPreKey`, `Session`, `DmKey`, `DpopKey`) implement `Zeroize` or `ZeroizeOnDrop`. Secret key bytes persist in memory after the structs are dropped and may be recovered from heap memory, core dumps, or swap. The `zeroize` crate is not even a dependency.

**Locations:**
- `/Users/chad/src/freeq/freeq-sdk/src/crypto.rs:26-29` - `PrivateKey` enum holds `SigningKey` without zeroize
- `/Users/chad/src/freeq/freeq-sdk/src/crypto.rs:147-152` - `secret_bytes()` returns a `Vec<u8>` of raw key material that is never zeroized
- `/Users/chad/src/freeq/freeq-sdk/src/x3dh.rs:68-72` - `IdentityKeyPair` stores `StaticSecret` with `Clone` (which copies key bytes)

# freeq-bots

AI agent bots that do real, observable work in IRC channels.

## Bots

### 🏭 Software Factory (`/factory`)
A multi-agent development team that builds software collaboratively in a channel. Agent roles:
- **Product Lead** — clarifies requirements, writes spec
- **Architect** — proposes design and stack
- **Builder** — writes code using real tools
- **Reviewer** — critiques quality and spec alignment
- **QA** — generates and runs tests
- **Deploy** — deploys to staging with live URL

### 🔍 Architecture Auditor (`/audit`)
Clones a GitHub repo, analyzes structure, and posts findings: system diagram, bottlenecks, coupling risks, and refactor suggestions.

### ⚡ Spec-to-Prototype (`/prototype`)
Drop in a product spec, get a deployed application back in minutes. From idea → live URL.

## Running

```bash
# Set your API key
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

# Run the bot
cargo run --release --bin freeq-bots -- \
  --server irc.freeq.at:6667 \
  --nick factory \
  --channel "#factory"
```

## Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `/factory build <spec>` | Start the full factory pipeline |
| `/factory status` | Current factory phase and project |

content.text:
Found the picture. "Entire" is a separate tool wired in via `.claude/settings.json` — it has 6 hooks (SessionStart, SessionEnd, Stop, UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse:Task, PostToolUse:Task/TodoWrite) and pushes `entire/checkpoints/v1` to your `origin` (github.com:chad/freeq.git). Here's the proposed plan — these include destructive remote ops, so I want to confirm before I run them.

**Plan:**

1. **Delete the remote branch** — `git push origin --delete entire/checkpoints/v1`
2. **Delete the local branch** — `git branch -D entire/checkpoints/v1`
3. **Prune local remote-tracking ref** — `git fetch --prune origin`
4. **Remove all `entire` hooks from `.claude/settings.json`** (6 hook entries) and drop the `Read(./.entire/metadata/**)` deny rule
5. *(Optional)* delete the on-disk `./.entire/` directory (where its metadata lives) — say yes/no

**Caveats you should know:**
- Pushing `--delete` removes the ref from GitHub immediately, but the underlying commit objects can linger as unreachable for ~90 days and are reachable to anyone who already cloned. If you believe real secrets were on that branch and want them gone from GitHub's storage, you'd need to contact GitHub Support to expedite GC. Based on my scan I didn't find real API-key/PEM hits — only placeholder mentions and the substantive content of your prompts/transcripts. So the tradeoff is: regular delete is fine for "stop publishing this", but not a guaranteed wipe.
- This won't uninstall the `entire` binary itself or touch any global `~/.claude/settings.json` — want me to also check global hooks?

content.text: I think I see it. For DMs, the buffer-resolution on inbound TAGMSG uses `from` — but when the server *echoes* my own TAGMSG back, `from = me` and `target = the peer`. So the reaction lands on a DM buffer keyed by my own nick instead of the peer's. Let me confirm with how regular DM PRIVMSGs handle echo.

content.text:
Diagnosis: it's a DM-specific bug (channel reactions route via `target.hasPrefix("#")` and work fine). When you send a reaction in a DM, the server echoes it back with `from = you, target = peer`, but the TAGMSG handlers do `target.hasPrefix("#") ? target : from` — for DMs this picks `from` (you), routing the reaction to a non-existent DM with yourself. The PRIVMSG path at line 1284 already has the correct pattern: `isSelf ? target : from`.

Same bug exists in the `+draft/delete` and `+freeq.at/unreact` handlers — your own deletes/unreactions in DMs won't show locally either.

fix(ios): route self-echo TAGMSG in DMs to peer's buffer, not sender's

in a DM were applied to a buffer keyed by their own nick (effectively invisible).

unaffected because `target` starts with `#`.

1	import XCTest
2	@testable import freeq
3	
4	/// Buffer-routing invariants: anything in `appState.channels` must look like
5	/// an IRC channel (`#` or `&` prefix); anything in `appState.dmBuffers` must
6	/// look like a peer nick (no channel prefix).
7	///
8	/// Real bug observed: a DM with an agent (`@yokota`) appeared in the Channels
9	/// pane. That can only happen if a non-channel name landed in `channels` —
10	/// either via a direct call to `getOrCreateChannel`, or via an event handler
11	/// that received a non-prefixed target from the wire (e.g. an agent path on
12	/// the server). The tests below exercise both directions.
13	final class BufferRoutingTests: XCTestCase {
14	
15	    private func makeState() -> AppState {
16	        // AppState() reads UserDefaults / Keychain in init. For tests we want
17	        // a clean slate per test, so blow those away first.
18	        for k in ["freeq.nick", "freeq.server", "freeq.channels", "freeq.readPositions",
19	                  "freeq.unreadCounts", "freeq.mutedChannels"] {
20	            UserDefaults.standard.removeObject(forKey: k)
21	        }
22	        return AppState()
23	    }
24	
25	    // MARK: - getOrCreateChannel must reject names that aren't channels
26	
27	    func testGetOrCreateChannelRejectsBareNick() {
28	        let s = makeState()
29	        // Pre-condition.
30	        XCTAssertTrue(s.channels.isEmpty)

content.text:
Committed. Here's what landed:

**Coverage (19 tests):**

| Pattern | Tests |
|---|---|
| **A. DM self-echo bug (the one you reported)** | reactions, unreactions, deletions — each verifying the event lands on the peer's DM and no phantom self-buffer is created |
| **A. Channel/remote regression guards** | remote react in DM, self-echo in channel, remote react in channel |
| **B. PRIVMSG path already correct — locked in** | PRIVMSG self-echo in DM, PRIVMSG edit self-echo in DM |
| **C. Typing self-filter pinned** | self-echo `+typing` must not show "you are typing" in your own DM |
| **Sweep invariants** | "no TAGMSG self-echo handler may ever produce a DM buffer keyed by my own nick" + same for PRIVMSG — these catch the *pattern* in any future handler |
| **Sequencing/edge** | toggle (react→unreact), idempotent duplicate, multi-emoji accumulation, case-mixed `from`, case-mixed `target` merges to existing buffer, react/delete of unknown msgid don't crash |

**Verified by negative test:** I reintroduced the bug locally (`bufferName = target.hasPrefix("#") ? target : from`) — 13 of 19 tests failed, and only the PRIVMSG/channel/typing-filter controls correctly stayed green. Then restored the fix → 19/19 pass.

**Cross-platform audit:**
- JS SDK (`freeq-sdk-js/src/client.ts:741, 885`) — already correct
- Rust TUI (`freeq-tui/src/main.rs:1064`) — already correct (and *also* optimistically emits on send)
- Rust SDK — just passes through `{from, target, tags}`; routing is the caller's job

So iOS was the only client with the bug, and the test suite now guards the pattern going forward.

**Two follow-ups worth knowing about (not bugs, but adjacent):**
1. TUI doesn't handle `+freeq.at/unreact` or `+draft/delete` at all — missing feature, not a routing bug.
2. iOS sends reactions via `appState.sendReaction(...)` which now relies entirely on the server's `echo-message` echo for local display. That works because `FreeqClient` negotiates the cap (`freeq-sdk/src/client.rs:1895`), but if a connection ever loses that cap, your own reactions silently disappear again. The JS SDK does belt-and-suspenders (optimistic local emit on send). Want me to add the same optimistic update to iOS as a defense?

Perfect. Now I have the full picture. Let me create a comprehensive structured analysis:

## iOS Authentication & Persistent Sessions: Complete Map

### 1. STORAGE LAYER

**Keychain (encrypted at rest, `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlock`):**
- **`brokerToken`** — Long-lived credential (90+ days unless PDS refresh revoked). Written by OAuth callback (freeqApp.swift:102), read on init (AppState.swift:420), cleared on logout (AppState.swift:615) and on PDS refresh fatal (AppState.swift:847).
- **`did`** — Authenticated DID. Written at OAuth callback (freeqApp.swift:108), on authenticated event (AppState.swift:1158), read on init (AppState.swift:417), cleared on logout (AppState.swift:614).
- **`webToken`** — SASL access token (30 min TTL). Written after broker session fetch (AppState.swift:489), read on init (AppState.swift:427), cleared on logout (AppState.swift:616) and when cached token expires (AppState.swift:435).

**UserDefaults (plaintext, not encrypted):**
- **`freeq.nick`** — Current username. Written at connect (AppState.swift:541), read on init (AppState.swift:386).
- **`freeq.webTokenExpiry`** — Expiry timestamp of cached web token (25 min conservative window vs 30 min server TTL). Written after broker fetch (AppState.swift:490), read on init (AppState.swift:428), cleared on logout (AppState.swift:617).
- **`freeq.lastLogin`** — Unix timestamp for the 14-day grace window. Written on authenticated event (AppState.swift:1160) and at OAuth callback (freeqApp.swift:101).
- **`freeq.handle`** — Display handle from OAuth. Written at callback (freeqApp.swift:100), read in ConnectView (ConnectView.swift:5).
- **`freeq.brokerBase`** — Auth broker URL (https://auth.freeq.at). Written at connect (AppState.swift:543), read on init (AppState.swift:423).
- **`freeq.channels`**, **`freeq.mutedChannels`**, **`freeq.unreadCounts`**, **`freeq.readPositions`**, **`freeq.darkTheme`** — UI state (non-critical).

**Keychain Helper (AppState.swift:414-415):** One-time migration from UserDefaults to Keychain on first launch post-upgrade.

---

### 2. COLD-LAUNCH PATH

**App entry (freeqApp.swift):**
1. `@main FreeqApp` creates `@StateObject private var appState = AppState()` (freeqApp.swift:39).
2. `AppState.init()` (AppState.swift:384) loads:
   - Broker token from Keychain → `brokerToken` property
   - DID from Keychain → `authenticatedDID` property
   - Cached web token (if not expired) → `cachedWebToken` + `cachedWebTokenExpiry`
   - Nick from UserDefaults
3. **Decision tree**: `hasSavedSession` property (AppState.swift:372) returns **true if and only if** `brokerToken != nil`. No other signals.
4. `ContentView` (imported via `FreeqApp` body) reads `appState.hasSavedSession`:
   - **If true**: Calls `reconnectSavedSession()` on first appearance → shows "Connecting…" overlay
   - **If false**: Shows `ConnectView` for OAuth login

**Scene phase handling (AppState.swift:946-971):**
- On `.active` (foreground): If `connectionState == .disconnected && hasSavedSession`, call `reconnectSavedSession()`. Skips broker round-trip if SDK still has live transport (FEAT-004).
- Clears reconnect retry count on foreground transition.

---

### 3. SASL FLOW & WEB TOKEN REQUIREMENTS

**FreeqClient (Rust SDK, FFI boundary):**
- Requires a web token set via `client?.setWebToken(token: token)` (AppState.swift:562).
- Web token is exchanged by the broker for the PDS refresh token + access token.
- SDK sends web token in SASL authentication against the IRC server.
- The IRC server validates the web token with the broker (server-side challenge signature verification is NOT used by iOS — only the broker's pre-minted web token).

**Reconnect flow (AppState.swift:457-523):**
1. Check if `pendingWebToken` already set from initial login → use immediately (line 462).
2. Check if `cachedWebToken` still valid (< 25 min old) → reuse (line 468).
3. Fetch fresh web token from broker via `fetchBrokerSession(brokerToken:)` (line 481).
4. On success: cache for 25 min, save to Keychain, call `connect(nick:)` with token.

---

### 4. WEB TOKEN & BROKER INTERACTION

**Broker (`freeq-auth-broker/src/main.rs`):**

**OAuth Flow:**
- `GET /auth/login?handle=...&mobile=1` → DID resolution → PAR flow → generates fresh DPoP key (line 557).
- Stores `PendingAuth` struct in memory (line 670-686) with DPoP key (base64-encoded) and nonce.
- OAuth callback redirects to `freeq://auth?token=...&broker_token=...&did=...&handle=...` (line 892).

**Session endpoint (`POST /session`):**
- iOS sends `{"broker_token": "..."}` (line 276, 804).
- Broker looks up stored refresh token in SQLite (line 1006-1044), decrypted with AES-256-GCM (line 1018-1028).
- Calls PDS token endpoint with DPoP proof + refresh token (line 1070-1107).
- If PDS returns 400/401 with nonce → retries once with nonce (line 1087-1102).
- Returns fresh access token + updated refresh token + updated nonce.
- Broker stores encrypted refresh token + DPoP key + nonce back to DB (line 954-958).
- Mints a fresh **web token** (30 min TTL) by calling freeq-server's `/auth/broker/web-token` endpoint (line 960, 1134-1163).
- Returns `BrokerSessionResponse` with `token`, `nick`, `did`, `handle` (line 998-1003).

**Refresh token storage:** The broker holds the **encrypted PDS refresh token** (long-lived, ~90 days). iOS never sees it. iOS holds the **broker_token** (also ~90 days or until PDS revokes the refresh).

---

### 5. INVALIDATION TRIGGERS & CREDENTIAL CLEARING

**Auth error logging (AppState.swift:7-11):** Diagnostic log for "credential-clearing events with decision inputs" to diagnose broker flapping.

**Consecutive 401 tracking (AppState.swift:782-910):**
- Counter increments on 401 from broker (line 872).
- **3-strike rule**: Clear credentials if ≥3 consecutive 401s **AND** user is >14 days past last login (line 882).
- **Escalation rule**: Clear immediately if ≥8 consecutive 401s, even within 14-day grace window (line 881).
- **Grace window**: If < 14 days since `freeq.lastLogin`, never auto-clear on transient 401s (line 879-880, 792-795).

**Fatal PDS errors (AppState.swift:831-851):**
- If broker returns 502/503/504 with body containing `"invalid_grant"`, `"invalid_token"`, `"expired"`, or `"revoked"` → immediately clear all credentials (line 842-850).
- Logs as `authLog.error("Clearing broker credentials: PDS refresh fatal...")` (line 842).

**Explicit logout (AppState.swift:611-627):**
- Clears broker token, web token, DID, nick.
- Resets cached token expiry to `.distantPast`.

**Token expiry on guest fallback (AppState.swift:1129-1145):**
- If expecting authenticated session but got `Guest` nick → invalidate cached token, retry via broker (line 1131-1143).

**Disconnection without auto-reconnect (AppState.swift:598-608):**
- Clears channels/DMs but **does not** clear credentials — allows reconnect on foreground.

---

### 6. DPoP NONCE & BROKER FLAPPING

**DPoP mechanism (freeq-auth-broker main.rs:91-134):**
- Broker generates fresh EC P-256 key per login (line 557).
- Initial PAR request without nonce → receives nonce in response header (line 589-593).
- On 400 status with nonce → retries once with nonce (line 595-630).
- Stores DPoP key (base64-encoded) in broker DB (line 851).

**Nonce reuse on refresh (main.rs:1057-1132):**
- On `POST /session`, broker retrieves stored nonce from DB (line 1024).
- Includes stored nonce on first token refresh attempt (line 1071).
- If 400/401 received → reads new nonce from response header (line 1079-1084).
- Retries once with new nonce (line 1087-1102).
- Updates stored nonce in DB (line 951).

**3-strike threshold interpretation:**
- **Strike 1-2**: Retry within the same broker session fetch (retry loop 0-3, line 799).
- **Strike 3+**: Across reconnect cycles (consecutive 401 counter increments per session fetch attempt, line 872).
- Reset on success (line 906).

---

### 7. TOKEN REFRESH ON APP LAUNCH

**NOT proactive.** Reactive only:
1. On `reconnectSavedSession()`: Check if cached token still valid (< 25 min old). If not → fetch fresh from broker.
2. No background refresh task.
3. If web token expires **during** an active IRC session → SASL fails → `authFailed` event (AppState.swift:1170) → shows error message. Does **not** auto-retry or force re-OAuth.

**Smoking gun**: A 30-min web token with only 25-min cache window means the app can go 5 min without reconnecting before the token is stale. On a cold launch after 5+ min in background, the app will fetch a fresh token. But if the broker is flapping or down, the 401s accumulate quickly → hits the 3-strike threshold within 2-3 reconnect attempts.

---

### 8. OAUTH IN-FLIGHT STATE & KEYCHAIN ACCESSIBILITY

**Bluesky OAuth refresh token:**
- **Stored in**: Broker DB (encrypted with AES-256-GCM), not in iOS Keychain.
- **Lost on**: App reinstall (new Keychain sandbox), iOS major version upgrade (Keychain may be reset), manual Keychain wipe.
- **iOS Keychain accessibility class** (KeychainHelper.swift:24): `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlock`.
  - Data is locked while device is locked.
  - Data survives app crashes, backgrounding, and device lock/unlock.
  - Data is lost on device factory reset, iOS update on some devices.

**Smoking guns for frequent re-signin:**
1. **14-day grace window too short**: If app backgrounded for 14+ days, next cold launch triggers credentials cleared on first 401 (even if transient). User must re-OAuth.
2. **Broker flapping**: If broker has intermittent downtime or DNS issues, 401s spike → 3-strike threshold hit in 2-3 attempts → forced re-OAuth.
3. **Nonce retry logic**: If DPoP nonce is lost (e.g., broker DB crash), every token refresh fails on first attempt, retries with new nonce. If broker is restarting frequently, this becomes a loop.
4. **No proactive refresh**: If user backgrounded app for exactly 25-34 min, cold launch will fetch a fresh token. But if broker is down → immediate 401 → cached token was already invalid → 1 strike. After 3 cold launches with broker down, credentials cleared.
5. **Cached token expiry in UserDefaults (plaintext)**: If device time jumps backward (NTP issue, timezone change), `Date()` comparison at line 431 may incorrectly think token is still valid, leading to SASL failures after cache hit.

---

## Recommended Diagnostic Steps

1. Check broker uptime/error logs for 502/503 and invalid_grant patterns.
2. Inspect `freeq.lastLogin` timestamp in UserDefaults — if it's very old, verify the 14-day grace window logic.
3. Monitor `consecutive401Count` increments — add telemetry to track how often this counter crosses thresholds.
4. Test NTP correctness and time-jump scenarios (device sleep/wake).
5. Verify Keychain accessibility class doesn't lose data on app background/foreground cycle (test with debugger breakpoints).

- **Grace window**: If < 14 days since `freeq.lastLogin`, never auto-clear on transient 401s (line 879-880, 792-795).

**Fatal PDS errors (AppState.swift:831-851):**
- If broker returns 502/503/504 with body containing `"invalid_grant"`, `"invalid_token"`, `"expired"`, or `"revoked"` → immediately clear all credentials (line 842-850).
- Logs as `authLog.error("Clearing broker credentials: PDS refresh fatal...")` (line 842).

**Explicit logout (AppState.swift:611-627):**
- Clears broker token, web token, DID, nick.
- Resets cached token expiry to `.distantPast`.

**Token expiry on guest fallback (AppState.swift:1129-1145):**
- If expecting authenticated session but got `Guest` nick → invalidate cached token, retry via broker (line 1131-1143).

**Disconnection without auto-reconnect (AppState.swift:598-608):**
- Clears channels/DMs but **does not** clear credentials — allows reconnect on foreground.

---

### 6. DPoP NONCE & BROKER FLAPPING

**DPoP mechanism (freeq-auth-broker main.rs:91-134):**
- Broker generates fresh EC P-256 key per login (line 557).
- Initial PAR request without nonce → receives nonce in response header (line 589-593).
- On 400 status with nonce → retries once with nonce (line 595-630).
- Stores DPoP key (base64-encoded) in broker DB (line 851).

**Nonce reuse on refresh (main.rs:1057-1132):**
- On `POST /session`, broker retrieves stored nonce from DB (line 1024).
- Includes stored nonce on first token refresh attempt (line 1071).
- If 400/401 received → reads new nonce from response header (line 1079-1084).
- Retries once with new nonce (line 1087-1102).
- Updates stored nonce in DB (line 951).

**3-strike threshold interpretation:**
- **Strike 1-2**: Retry within the same broker session fetch (retry loop 0-3, line 799).
- **Strike 3+**: Across reconnect cycles (consecutive 401 counter increments per session fetch attempt, line 872).
- Reset on success (line 906).

---

### 7. TOKEN REFRESH ON APP LAUNCH

**NOT proactive.** Reactive only:
1. On `reconnectSavedSession()`: Check if cached token still valid (< 25 min old). If not → fetch fresh from broker.
2. No background refresh task.
3. If web token expires **during** an active IRC session → SASL fails → `authFailed` event (AppState.swift:1170) → shows error message. Does **not** auto-retry or force re-OAuth.

**Smoking gun**: A 30-min web token with only 25-min cache window means the app can go 5 min without reconnecting before the token is stale. On a cold launch after 5+ min in background, the app will fetch a fresh token. But if the broker is flapping or down, the 401s accumulate quickly → hits the 3-strike threshold within 2-3 reconnect attempts.

---

### 8. OAUTH IN-FLIGHT STATE & KEYCHAIN ACCESSIBILITY

**Bluesky OAuth refresh token:**
- **Stored in**: Broker DB (encrypted with AES-256-GCM), not in iOS Keychain.
- **Lost on**: App reinstall (new Keychain sandbox), iOS major version upgrade (Keychain may be reset), manual Keychain wipe.
- **iOS Keychain accessibility class** (KeychainHelper.swift:24): `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlock`.
  - Data is locked while device is locked.
  - Data survives app crashes, backgrounding, and device lock/unlock.
  - Data is lost on device factory reset, iOS update on some devices.

**Smoking guns for frequent re-signin:**
1. **14-day grace window too short**: If app backgrounded for 14+ days, next cold launch triggers credentials cleared on first 401 (even if transient). User must re-OAuth.
2. **Broker flapping**: If broker has intermittent downtime or DNS issues, 401s spike → 3-strike threshold hit in 2-3 attempts → forced re-OAuth.
3. **Nonce retry logic**: If DPoP nonce is lost (e.g., broker DB crash), every token refresh fails on first attempt, retries with new nonce. If broker is restarting frequently, this becomes a loop.
4. **No proactive refresh**: If user backgrounded app for exactly 25-34 min, cold launch will fetch a fresh token. But if broker is down → immediate 401 → cached token was already invalid → 1 strike. After 3 cold launches with broker down, credentials cleared.
5. **Cached token expiry in UserDefaults (plaintext)**: If device time jumps backward (NTP issue, timezone change), `Date()` comparison at line 431 may incorrectly think token is still valid, leading to SASL failures after cache hit.

---

## Recommended Diagnostic Steps

1. Check broker uptime/error logs for 502/503 and invalid_grant patterns.
2. Inspect `freeq.lastLogin` timestamp in UserDefaults — if it's very old, verify the 14-day grace window logic.
3. Monitor `consecutive401Count` increments — add telemetry to track how often this counter crosses thresholds.
4. Test NTP correctness and time-jump scenarios (device sleep/wake).
5. Verify Keychain accessibility class doesn't lose data on app background/foreground cycle (test with debugger breakpoints).
Let me verify the most damning bits by reading the actual code, especially the auto-wipe logic — that's the most likely smoking gun.

content.text:
I have the full picture. Here's the deep analysis.

## What you're actually complaining about: two problems, not one

When you say "have to sign in again" plus "should immediately get to main UI with channels and DMs", that's **two separate failures** that look the same from the seat:

1. **You actually got logged out** — `brokerToken` was wiped from Keychain → `hasSavedSession == false` → ConnectScreen.
2. **You're still logged in but staring at "Connecting…"** — every cold launch shows a spinner until SASL+JOIN+CHATHISTORY all complete, because **iOS has no local message cache**. The UI is empty until the network round-trips finish.

Fixing only #1 won't make the experience feel right. You need both.

---

## Problem 1: Why credentials get wiped

Three triggers, all in `AppState.swift`:

### A. The 8-strike escalation rule (line 881)
```
let escalated = count >= 8
let shouldClear = (count >= 3 && !withinGrace) || escalated
```
Eight consecutive 401s from the broker — across reconnect cycles, **ignoring the 14-day grace window** — wipes credentials. Each reconnect cycle can rack 4 strikes (retries 0–3 in the inner loop, line 799). So **2 unlucky reconnects with a flapping broker** is enough to log you out, even on day 1.

### B. The 5xx-body keyword wipe (lines 831–851)
```swift
let isRefreshFatal = body.contains("invalid_grant")
    || body.contains("invalid_token")
    || body.contains("expired")
    || body.contains("revoked")
```
On 502/503/504, the iOS app scans the response *body* for those four keywords. If the broker (or a CDN/proxy in front of it) returns a 5xx whose body happens to contain "expired" or "revoked" anywhere — including stack traces, generic error pages, or unrelated text — credentials are wiped **immediately**, no retry. This is dangerously brittle.

### C. The Guest-fallback invalidation (lines 1131–1145)
If SASL succeeds but the server ends up registering you as `guest…` while `authenticatedDID != nil`, the cached web token gets nuked, broker is forced to refresh. This is fine *once*, but if the broker also fails, you accumulate strikes via path A.

### D. The 25-min cache for a 30-min token
On any cold launch ≥ 25 min after the last connection, you **always** hit the broker. Every broker hit is a chance to trip A or B. There's no proactive refresh while the app is alive.

---

## Problem 2: Why "Connecting…" instead of channels on cold launch

`ContentView.swift:11` switches on `connectionState`:
- `.disconnected` + `hasSavedSession` → **spinner** with 12s/20s/45s escalating copy.
- `.connecting` → **spinner**.
- `.connected`/`.registered` → MainTabView.

So even when your session is healthy, you watch the spinner through:
1. Read brokerToken from Keychain (fast)
2. Check cached webToken expiry (usually expired on cold launch)
3. POST /session to auth.freeq.at — **network round-trip**, must succeed
4. `setWebToken` + `connect()` → WebSocket handshake
5. CAP/SASL exchange
6. JOIN every saved channel
7. CHATHISTORY requests come back
8. UI finally has messages to render

There is **no local SQLite/CoreData/file-based message cache**. Messages live only in `@Published` arrays in memory. Cold launch = empty buffers until the network completes.

`freeq-app` (web) has `lib/db.ts` for local persistence. iOS has nothing equivalent.

---

## What "almost never sign in again" actually requires

Ranked by impact and effort. I'd want your direction on priority before I touch any of this.

### Tier 1 — Stop the bleeding (small, high-leverage)

**1. Kill the 8-strike escalation, or push it to 50+.** Eight 401s is nothing on a flaky network. The 14-day grace window already exists; the escalation defeats it. (`AppState.swift:881`)

**2. Remove the body-keyword wipe, or require both a structured PDS error code *and* a long retry window.** Today's "string contains 'expired'" check on a 5xx body is a footgun. The signal we want — "PDS told us the refresh token is genuinely dead" — should come from the **broker** as a discrete status code or JSON field, not iOS guessing from text. (`AppState.swift:831–851`)

**3. Don't let any wipe path bypass the 14-day grace.** Within 14 days of `lastLogin`, nothing automatic should clear credentials. Explicit logout only.

These three changes alone probably eliminate 80% of the unwanted sign-outs.

### Tier 2 — Make cold launch feel instant

**4. Local message cache (SQLite via GRDB or just JSON files).** On launch, hydrate `channels`/`dmBuffers` from disk *before* the connection completes. Show MainTabView the instant `hasSavedSession` is true, with a small "Connecting…" pill in the chrome instead of a full-screen spinner. New messages stream in from CHATHISTORY when the connection lands.

**5. Decouple UI gating from `connectionState`.** Today: `.connected/.registered → MainTabView`. Should be: `hasSavedSession → MainTabView` (always), with connection state as a banner/indicator. ConnectScreen only when there are genuinely no credentials. The "Connecting…" full-screen is appropriate for a *first* sign-in, not for every cold launch.

**6. Proactive token refresh.** When the app foregrounds with a healthy connection but the cached web token has <5 min left, kick off a background refresh. Don't let the cache *ever* go fully stale while the app is alive.

### Tier 3 — Make the broker dance more resilient

**7. Extend the web-token cache to ~28 min (or whatever the server hands back as TTL) and trust it.** The conservative 25/30 just adds broker load.

**8. Bound the cold-launch broker call with a short timeout (3s) and fall back to a "limited mode" UI** — viewable cached messages, send disabled, retry banner. Today a slow broker means a full-screen spinner indefinitely.

**9. Keychain accessibility audit.** `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlock` is correct (survives reboots, available in background). Worth confirming the data isn't synced to iCloud Keychain accidentally (that has different lifetime semantics), and that the access group hasn't drifted across app updates. If you've ever changed bundle ID or signing identity, the Keychain entries are orphaned and you'd silently relogin once per upgrade.

### Tier 4 — Bigger lifts (only if Tier 1–3 isn't enough)

**10. Background refresh task (BGAppRefreshTask).** Have iOS wake the app every few hours to refresh the web token and pull CHATHISTORY incrementally. Then a cold launch finds a warm token + fresh messages.

**11. Move broker call earlier in the launch.** Right now `reconnectSavedSession()` fires from `onAppear` of `reconnectingView`. Could fire from `AppState.init()` so the network call overlaps with SwiftUI hydration.

---

## My recommendation

Do Tier 1 + #4 + #5 from Tier 2. That's the smallest set that turns "I get logged out" and "I stare at a spinner" into "the app opens to my channels every time, even on a slow network."

Want me to start? If yes, which order — Tier 1 first (smallest, biggest stop-the-bleeding wins), or the local cache first (most visible UX change)?

git add freeq-ios/freeq/Models/AppState.swift && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(ios): never clear credentials on 5xx — treat as transient broker error

The previous logic substring-searched broker 502/503/504 response bodies
for 'invalid_grant' / 'invalid_token' / 'expired' / 'revoked' and wiped
credentials immediately on any match. That was brittle: a CDN error page,
a stack trace, or any unrelated text containing those words silently
logged the user out.

5xx is a server error, not an authorization verdict. All 502/503/504 are
now treated as transient — retried inside the fetch and then surfaced to
the outer reconnect loop for backoff. Credentials are untouched.

If the PDS genuinely refuses the refresh, that should surface as a 401
from the broker (and run through the 3-strikes-past-grace path). The
right long-term fix is a structured discriminator from the broker
(specific status or JSON field) signaling "refresh invalidated, re-auth
required" — but text-sniffing the body was the wrong way to get there.
EOF
)"

content.text:
Pausing here for a sanity check before continuing. Five commits on the branch since you said go:

| Commit | What changed |
|---|---|
| `5b89538` | Tier 1.1 — removed 8-strike escalation that bypassed the grace window |
| `e48d4ca` | Tier 1.2 — removed 5xx body-keyword wipe; 5xx is now always transient |
| `4fae618` | Tier 2.4 — local buffer cache at App Support, file-protected, hydrated in `init()` |
| `b5ce9a8` | Tier 2.5 — ContentView now gates on `hasSavedSession`, not `connectionState`; status banner replaces full-screen spinner |
| `5b08184` | test hygiene — clear `BufferCacheStore` between tests |

**What the experience should feel like now:**
- Cold launch: instant MainTabView with your last channels/DMs and the most recent ~50 messages per buffer, plus a thin "Connecting…" pill at the top that disappears when SASL completes. The wire CHATHISTORY merges in seamlessly via `appendIfNew`.
- A flapping broker no longer logs you out. Within 14 days of last login, **nothing** automatic clears credentials. Past 14 days, only 3 sustained 401s (server-side) do.

feat(ios): proactive web-token refresh on foreground when within 10 min of expiry

Cached web tokens live ~25 min (server TTL ~30 min). Without proactive
refresh, a WebSocket that survives backgrounding but drops 25+ min later
forces the reconnect to block on the broker. On a slow or flaky broker
that's a multi-second user-visible delay.

handleScenePhase(.active) now triggers a background refresh when the
cached token has <10 min of life left AND the SDK transport is still
healthy. The refresh runs detached from the UI — failures stay silent
(the token is still valid for several more minutes; the next genuine
reconnect retries via the standard path).

Single-flight via `proactiveRefreshInFlight` so a rapid foreground/
background cycle doesn't fire concurrent broker calls.
EOF
